Abstract 
30 
Extended cessation (EC), an advanced meditative state in which consciousness is volitionally 
31 
suspended and later reset with immense mental clarity, equanimity, and peace, offers an 
32 
endogenous model for investigating the mechanisms of consciousness. Using ultra-high-resolution 
33 
7T fMRI with dense within-subject sampling (N=3), we quantified whole-brain activity, functional 
34 
and effective connectivity, cortical gradients, and eigenmodes, and related them to 
35 
chemoarchitecture and cognitive maps. EC is marked by increased activity in unimodal regions, 
36 
down-regulation in transmodal regions, subcortex, and brainstem, an expansion of the principal 
37 
gradient, and decrease in low-order global eigenmodes. Cognitive decoding linked EC to 
38 
heightened perceptual clarity and attention, least with mental suffering, and co-varied with 
39 
histaminergic H₃ receptors topology. These findings challenge predictions of Global Neuronal 
40 
Workspace and Integrated Information Theory, while supporting the Active Inference Framework. 
41 
More broadly, EC demonstrates that consciousness can cease without global suppression, 
42

40 
Workspace and Integrated Information Theory, while supporting the Active Inference Framework. 
41 
More broadly, EC demonstrates that consciousness can cease without global suppression, 
42 
suggesting a potential “reset” mechanism that fosters equanimity and the potential for flourishing.

45

Introduction 
48 
In most scientific and clinical frameworks, wakefulness is assumed to entail ongoing 
49 
subjective consciousness1. Advanced meditative states challenge this assumption by showing that 
50 
consciousness can be radically thinned to minimal ‘content-free’ awareness2,3 or even entirely 
51 
suspended momentarily4-7 or for an extended duration8. These findings highlight the extraordinary 
52 
plasticity of consciousness, raising fundamental questions for theories of consciousness, including 
53 
Global Neuronal Workspace, Integrated Information Theory, and Active Inferences Framework, 
54 
and their relevance to human flourishing9,10. 
55 
Advanced meditation situates these rare states within a broader scientific agenda: 
56 
understanding consciousness as a trainable and reconfigurable capacity across meditative 
57 
development11,12. This systematic mental training can lead to meditative endpoints, variously 
58 
described as “awakening”13,14, “beatitude”15, “salvation”16, or “enlightenment”13,14, that entail 
59 
profound shifts in perception, emotion, and self-processing. Studying such endpoints offers not 
60

58 
described as “awakening”13,14, “beatitude”15, “salvation”16, or “enlightenment”13,14, that entail 
59 
profound shifts in perception, emotion, and self-processing. Studying such endpoints offers not 
60 
only a natural experiment for testing theories of consciousness but also insights into how such they 
61 
can induce a “reset” of consciousness that fosters equanimity, resilience, and well-being, all of 
62 
which are critical priorities in global mental health17-19. 
63 
Here, we study extended cessation (EC), a rare meditative endpoint where practitioners 
64 
report temporary suspensions of ordinary consciousness and mental activities4,8. EC can vary from 
65 
minutes to days, is entered and exited volitionally, and is frequently followed by profound 
66 
psychological aftereffects, including extreme sense of relief, clarity, openness, peace, equanimity, 
67 
heightened sensory sensitivity, significant reduction in mental suffering, reduced repetitive 
68 
thoughts, stoppage of negative self-talk, and an absence of inner narration8,18,19. Unlike non-
69 
conscious states induced by pharmacological agents (e.g., anesthesia or psychedelics)20,21, EC is 
70

68 
thoughts, stoppage of negative self-talk, and an absence of inner narration8,18,19. Unlike non-
69 
conscious states induced by pharmacological agents (e.g., anesthesia or psychedelics)20,21, EC is 
70 
unique in being endogenous, reversible, precisely timed, and followed by heightened well-being, 
71 
making it an excellent natural experiment for probing the mechanisms of consciousness. One 
72 
canonical form of EC found in Theravada Buddhism is Nirodha Samāpatti8,22-24. Here, we 
73 
investigate the broader neurobiological principles of EC without making definitive claims about 
74 
its doctrinal interpretation4,6,7. Please refer to Laukkonen et al. for a recent discussion of the 
75 
historical and theoretical context of Nirodha Samāpatti8. It is noteworthy that Nirodha Samāpatti

78 
From a neuroscience perspective, studying EC offers a powerful opportunity to explore the 
79 
fundamental nature and mechanisms of consciousness. A central limitation in current 
80 
consciousness research is the absence of an endogenous and reversible method to suspend 
81 
awareness while preserving physiological stability20,21 and enabling prospectively timed re-
82 
emergence8,23. EC addresses this gap7,8. Compared to sleep, anesthesia, and disorders of 
83 
consciousness, EC constitutes a fourth experimental context in its uniqueness self-initiate and 
84 
control consciousness. This enables precise examination of transition dynamics at entry and exit 
85 
and the mechanisms that dismantle and reinstate conscious access. Additionally, the notable 
86 
aftereffects of EC described above may relate to significant neuroplasticity and reorganization of 
87 
the brain, potentially informing techniques to enhance cognitive function, emotional regulation, 
88 
overall wellbeing and happiness, and thriving. 
89

87

88 
overall wellbeing and happiness, and thriving. 
89 
Current competing frameworks of consciousness make testable predictions for EC. Global 
90 
Neuronal Workspace (GNW) predicts a collapse of long-range connectivity and reduced feedback 
91 
connectivity during suspension of consciousness27 while Integrated Information Theory (IIT) 
92 
predicts a decrease in network differentiation during non-conscious states28. In contrast, the Active 
93 
Inference Framework (AIF) predicts down-weighting of sensory precision via thalamo-cortical 
94 
gating during non-conscious states29. The distinct features of EC allow comparison among theories 
95 
about how consciousness is represented and suspended in the human brain. 
96 
Here we present the first neuroscientific, empirical investigation of EC, using ultra-high-
97 
resolution 7T functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate the whole-brain 
98 
activity, connectivity, and causal dynamics of network organization during EC. The ultra-high 
99

97 
resolution 7T functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate the whole-brain 
98 
activity, connectivity, and causal dynamics of network organization during EC. The ultra-high 
99 
spatial resolution of 7T imaging allows for precise measurement of small but critical brain 
100 
structures that are central to consciousness and arousal regulation, such as the thalamus, brainstem, 
101 
and subcortical nuclei. This methodology is particularly powerful for the study of consciousness, 
102 
where transitions between conscious and non-conscious brain states may be mediated by subtle 
103 
shifts in deep-brain activity. Moreover, we used a novel and systematic neurophenomenological 
104

105

106 
This study provides important initial evidence for the neuroscience of EC and related 
107 
fundamental aspects of consciousness, with potential therapeutic implications for human 
108 
flourishing. In this study we sought to answer three key questions: (1) What brain reconfiguration 
109 
accompanies voluntary suspension of consciousness?; (2) What are the causal brain dynamics of 
110 
EC; and (3) Which neurochemical systems are implicated, as inferred from spatial coupling to 
111 
normative receptor distributions?  
112 
Our findings indicate that consciousness can be suspended without global cortical 
113 
suppression. During EC, unimodal systems (e.g., visual and dorsal-attention networks) remain 
114 
selectively engaged while transmodal hubs and thalamo-striatal circuits are down-regulated; the 
115 
principal cortical gradient polarizes rather than flattens; and network–neurochemical coupling 
116 
aligns with histaminergic (H₃) precision control of sensory gating. Meta-analytic decoding 
117

115 
principal cortical gradient polarizes rather than flattens; and network–neurochemical coupling 
116 
aligns with histaminergic (H₃) precision control of sensory gating. Meta-analytic decoding 
117 
demonstrated that EC is functionally aligned with perception and attention, while being least 
118 
associated with mental suffering and distress. This neuronal signature is most consistent with AIF, 
119 
constrains GNW theory, and challenges IIT. This study demonstrates that the human brain can 
120 
volitionally dismantle and reassemble conscious processing, offers a radical model for testing 
121 
theories of consciousness, and highlights the possibility that consciousness can ‘reset’ and orient 
122 
toward equanimity and flourishing.

Onset. All participants reported that internal verbalization (thinking), body sensations, and 
137 
feeling ceased during the onset of EC. With one exception, participants reported no primary 
138

139

141 
completely offline. 
142 
Exit. Upon emerging from EC, all participates reported that biographical memory and ego-
143 
centered cognitive processing were typically absent, and they did not perceive any separation 
144 
between themselves and the environment. All participants reported a vivid clarity of mind and an

146 
in a specific sequence: first thought, then bodily sensation, and finally internal verbalization. 
147 
Afterglow. After EC, all participants reported typically experiencing intense sensory 
148 
vividness, accompanied by a strong and lasting peace. They all also reported typically being 
149 
inclined to solitude, and many described a deep, abiding joy.

Neurosynth data-driven cognitive terms decoding analysis 
269 
Across both counting and memory control conditions. Correlation analysis revealed that 
270 
EC was positively associated with action, attention, fixation, gaze, planning, spatial attention, 
271 
visual attention, and visual perception. These terms are associated with perception and planning. 
272 
In contrast, negative associations were found with terms such as anxiety, arousal, eating, emotion, 
273

Discussion 
280 
Using ultra-high-resolution 7T functional MRI, we present the first neural characterization 
281 
of an extraordinary non-conscious state of extended cessation (EC). We used an intensive within-
282 
subject, dense-sampling design (N=3) , analogous to the rigorous small-N primate study designs 
283 
and small-N human case studies to prioritize internal validity and depth of individual-level 
284 
analysis36,37.  
285 
Human consciousness is supported by integrated brain activity across functionally 
286 
segregated areas38. Here we show that EC exhibits a distinctive reorganization of brain networks: 
287 
higher brain activity in unimodal brain systems (VN, DAN), and temporal pol, lower activity in 
288 
transmodal brain systems (DMN), as well as regions in the subcortex, brainstem, and cerebellum, 
289 
expansion of the principal cortical gradient, enhancing segregation between unimodal and 
290 
transmodal regions, and network–neurochemical coupling aligned with histaminergic H₃ receptor

292 
processes associated with perception/attention and reduced mental suffering and psychological

Whole-brain effective connectivity was assessed using regression dynamic causal 
494 
modeling (rDCM). rDCM, a variant of DCM for fMRI, enables effective connectivity analyses 
495 
across whole-brain networks with computational efficiency67-71 without sacrificing reliability72. 
496 
The validity of this procedure has been established in both task-based and resting-state fMRI 
497 
studies67,68,70,72,73. Specifically, rDCM incorporates modifications to DCM72 including: (1) 
498 
translation of equations into the frequency domain using Fourier transformation; (2) replacement 
499 
of nonlinear hemodynamic models with linear functions; (3) mean-field approximation across 
500 
regions; and (4) specification of priors for neuronal parameters and noise precision. These 
501 
modifications transform linear DCM into Bayesian linear regression in the frequency domain, 
502 
which ultimately facilitates highly efficient DCM model inference70,73.

532

(e.g. left/right hemisphere, anterior/posterior regions) while higher-number modes correspond to
